subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank, US State Dept, HRW, RSF, Freedom House) + international press; state media EXCLUDED on governance.

A high-income tourism economy in acute external/debt distress - reserves fell to ~$440m against far larger 2025-26 obligations, a first-ever sovereign sukuk default narrowly averted by Indian support, with twin 2024 downgrades.

Standout drags: acute debt distress, democratic backsliding (a Sept 2025 media-control law, protests), severe migrant-worker abuse, an India-China geopolitical whipsaw, and an existential climate threat as the world's lowest-lying nation (~80% under 1m).

economic.notes: A luxury-tourism economy in acute debt distress with a dangerous bank-sovereign nexus.

fiscal_state.notes: Acute distress - debt service ~70% of revenue by 2026 and reserves critically thin; default narrowly avoided via Indian support, with reform potential but extreme near-term vulnerability. (World Bank Oct 2025; IMF Art IV 2025)

monetary_independence.notes: Minimal - the rufiyaa is a managed/effectively USD-pegged regime under severe reserve pressure constraining MMA policy. (IMF)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The rufiyaa is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Moderate (~4.6% in 2025) but with FX-shortage and pass-through risk if the peg strains. (World Bank Oct 2025; IMF)

banking_stability.notes: A dangerous bank-sovereign nexus (sovereign exposure ~38-67% of assets) flagged by the IMF as systemic. (IMF Art IV 2025)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Near-total reliance on luxury tourism and imports, with almost no diversification and a chronic external deficit. (World Bank; IMF)

institutional.notes: Eroding rule of law and a sharply deteriorating press, with reasonable data.

rule_of_law.notes: Eroding - weakened parliament and independent commissions, politicized institutions under Muizzu. (HRW 2025; Freedom House 2025)

statistical_integrity.notes: Reasonable and IMF-engaged, though fiscal-data optimism gaps have been flagged. (IMF; Maldives Independent)

civil_service_capacity.notes: A functional administration delivering services, but strained by the fiscal crisis and politicization. (World Bank)

press_freedom.notes: Deteriorating sharply - a Sept 2025 media-control law, escalating attacks, RSF 104/180, and impunity for Islamist-gang threats. (HRW May 2026; RSF 2025)

political_social.notes: Volatile transitions and backsliding, with severe migrant-worker abuse and conservative pressure.

political_stability.notes: A history of volatile transitions (2008-2018) and Sept-Nov 2025 protests over corruption/backsliding amid a backsliding executive. (Wikipedia 2025 protests; CFR)

civil_liberties.notes: Narrowing space, with creeping Islamist-conservative pressure and constrained dissent. (Freedom House 2025; HRW)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Severe - ~100k documented + ~60k undocumented (mostly Bangladeshi) migrants face forced-labour indicators (passport confiscation, wage theft); protesters arrested/deported. (US State Dept; HRW 2024)

social_cohesion.notes: Religiously homogeneous and cohesive among citizens, but a sharp citizen/migrant divide and rising conservative pressure. (Freedom House; HRW)

demographics.notes: A young population, but heavy dependence on a large, marginalized foreign labour force. (World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: A volatile India-out/India-in whipsaw amid India-China contestation, with FX strains.

alliance_reliability.notes: Volatile - an 'India Out' pivot to China (2023) then back to India (2025), an unpredictable partner caught in India-China rivalry. (ORF; Lowy)

conflict_involvement.notes: No armed conflict, but a strategically contested Indian-Ocean location and a latent extremism risk. (observed; HRW on gangs)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: No sanctions, but acute FX shortages and capital/import-payment strains amid the reserve crisis. (IMF; World Bank)

physical_practical.notes: An existential climate threat as the world's lowest-lying nation, with debt crowding out social spend.

climate.notes: Existential - the lowest-lying nation on Earth; sea-level rise threatens habitability despite adaptation spend. (IPCC AR6; ND-GAIN)

healthcare.notes: Decent for the region but squeezed by debt service crowding out social spending. (World Bank Oct 2025)

infrastructure.notes: Good tourism/airport infrastructure, but dispersed-atoll logistics costs and debt-funded projects under strain. (World Bank)

crime_safety.notes: Generally safe for tourists, with gang violence, radicalization, and drug issues in Male. (observed; HRW)

disaster_insurance.notes: Extreme climate exposure with minimal insurability and almost no fiscal headroom. (inference; World Bank)

summary: A high-income luxury-tourism economy gripped by an acute external and debt crisis: reserves fell to roughly $440m against far larger 2025-26 obligations, twin 2024 downgrades cut market access, and a first-ever sovereign sukuk default was only narrowly averted by Indian support (a $400m swap and $565m credit line). Governance is backsliding - a September 2025 media-control law, attacks on the press, weakened oversight bodies, and large anti-corruption protests - alongside severe abuse of its large Bangladeshi migrant workforce and creeping Islamist-conservative pressure. Geopolitically it whipsawed from Muizzu's 'India Out'/pro-China tilt to renewed reliance on India in 2025. Above all, as the world's lowest-lying nation it faces an existential long-run climate threat, making the overall skew firmly negative.
